stem: The heights (in inches) of five plants are $6, 9, 11, 14, 18$. What is the range of the data?
explanation: Range equals maximum minus minimum: $18 - 6 = 12$.

stem: A data set has minimum $4$, $Q_1 = 7$, median $10$, $Q_3 = 15$, maximum $22$. What is the interquartile range?
explanation: IQR is $Q_3 - Q_1 = 15 - 7 = 8$. It ignores the min and max entirely.

stem: Data set $X$ has values clustered tightly around its mean, while data set $Y$ has values spread far from its mean. Both have the same mean. Which statement must be true?

choices.text: $X$ has a larger standard deviation than $Y$.
$X$ and $Y$ have the same standard deviation.
$Y$ has a larger standard deviation than $X$.

explanation: Standard deviation measures typical distance from the mean, so a more spread-out data set has a larger SD.

stem: Which measure of spread is most affected by a single extreme outlier?
explanation: Range depends entirely on the max and min, so one extreme value changes it dramatically. IQR resists outliers; median and mode are not measures of spread.

stem: The daily temperatures (in $^{\circ}\text{F}$) for a week were $62, 64, 63, 65, 64, 66, 63$. Without calculating, which description best fits the standard deviation of this data set?

choices.text: Very large, because temperatures vary a lot.
Very small, because the values are clustered close together.

explanation: All values sit within $4^{\circ}$ of each other, so typical distance from the mean is small. Standard deviation is never negative.

stem: A quiz score data set has $Q_1 = 12$ and $Q_3 = 19$. What is the interquartile range?
Every value in a data set is identical: $7, 7, 7, 7, 7$. What is the standard deviation?
explanation: Every value equals the mean, so each deviation is $0$ and the standard deviation is $0$.

stem: Class $A$ scored $70, 72, 74, 76, 78$ on a test. Class $B$ scored $60, 68, 74, 80, 88$. Both classes have a mean of $74$. Which statement is true?

choices.text: Class $B$ has a larger standard deviation than Class $A$.
Class $A$ has a larger standard deviation than Class $B$.
The two classes have equal standard deviations.
Standard deviation cannot be compared without more data.

explanation: Class $B$'s scores are spread much farther from the mean than Class $A$'s tightly clustered scores, so Class $B$ has the larger SD.

stem: A set of exam scores has minimum $55$, $Q_1 = 68$, median $75$, $Q_3 = 82$, and maximum $94$. If the maximum score of $94$ is replaced with $99$, which measure of spread will change?
explanation: Replacing only the max changes the range (max $-$ min) but leaves $Q_1$ and $Q_3$ unchanged, so IQR is unaffected.

stem: Data set $P$: $10, 11, 12, 13, 14$. Data set $Q$: $10, 11, 12, 13, 14, 50$. Compared to set $P$, the standard deviation of set $Q$ is:

choices.text: The same, because the other values are unchanged.
Smaller, because there are more values.
Larger, because $50$ is far from the others.
Zero, because adding a value averages out the spread.

explanation: The value $50$ is a major outlier, increasing the typical distance from the mean and therefore the SD.

stem: A researcher wants to describe the spread of household incomes in a neighborhood that includes a few very wealthy families. Which measure of spread is least distorted by those extreme incomes?
explanation: IQR uses only the middle 50% of values, so it is resistant to extreme outliers. Range, SD, and MAD all incorporate every value, including the extremes.

stem: Two data sets each have mean $50$. Set $X$: $48, 49, 50, 51, 52$. Set $Y$: $40, 45, 50, 55, 60$. Which statement is true about their spread?

choices.text: Set $X$ has a larger range and a larger SD.
Set $Y$ has a larger range but a smaller SD.
Both sets have identical ranges and SDs.
Set $Y$ has a larger range and a larger SD.

explanation: Set $Y$'s range is $20$ vs. $4$ for $X$, and its values sit farther from the mean, so its SD is also larger.

stem: A data set of nine values has $Q_1 = 14$ and $Q_3 = 26$. A tenth value of $100$ is added. Which of the following best describes the impact on the IQR?
explanation: IQR depends on the middle 50% of the data, so a single extreme outlier has little effect on $Q_1$ and $Q_3$.

stem: Every value in a data set is increased by $5$. Which measure of spread changes?
explanation: Adding a constant shifts every value equally, so distances between values are preserved. Range, SD, and IQR all stay the same.

stem: The ten lowest values in a data set are $12, 14, 15, 15, 17, 19, 20, 22, 25, 31$. What is the range of these ten values?
A teacher compares two class sections. Section $1$ scores: $80, 82, 84, 86, 88$. Section $2$ scores: $60, 75, 84, 92, 109$. Which statement is supported by the data?

choices.text: Section $1$ has a higher mean and a larger spread.
Section $2$ has greater variability than Section $1$.
Both sections have identical standard deviations.

explanation: Section $2$'s range is $49$ vs. $8$ for Section $1$, and its values deviate far more from the center, indicating greater variability.

stem: Two data sets each contain $20$ values and share the same median and IQR. Set $A$ has range $40$ and set $B$ has range $120$. Which conclusion is best supported?

choices.text: Set $A$ has a larger standard deviation than set $B$.
Set $B$ has more extreme values in its tails than set $A$.
The two sets must have the same mean.
Set $A$ contains more outliers than set $B$.

explanation: Same IQR means the middle 50% is similarly spread, but a much larger range in $B$ must come from values in the tails being farther from the center.

stem: Every value in a data set is multiplied by $3$. How does this affect the standard deviation and IQR?

choices.text: SD is multiplied by $9$; IQR by $3$.

explanation: Multiplying every value by a constant $k$ scales all distances between values by $|k|$, so SD and IQR are each multiplied by $3$.

stem: A company reports that two branches had the same mean weekly sales, but branch $X$ had a standard deviation of $\$2{,}000$ while branch $Y$ had a standard deviation of $\$8{,}000$. Which conclusion must be true?

choices.text: Branch $Y$ sold more total product than branch $X$.
Branch $Y$ had a higher median than branch $X$.
Branch $X$ had more weeks of zero sales than branch $Y$.
Branch $X$'s weekly sales were more consistent than branch $Y$'s.

explanation: A smaller SD with the same mean indicates weekly values were closer to the mean, i.e., more consistent. The other options don't follow from SD alone.

stem: A data set of $15$ values has range $30$, IQR $10$, and standard deviation $6$. Every value in the data set is then doubled and increased by $4$. What are the new range, IQR, and SD?
explanation: Adding a constant doesn't change spread; multiplying by $2$ scales range, IQR, and SD each by $2$, giving $60$, $20$, and $12$.
